text: up. In his detailed construction of the event, Zerilli (1998a) intimates that they stayed away on purpose, wanting to set their own agenda. This again was part of a European struggle over hegemony that would soon be fought with real weapons. Van Gennep was aiming to establish a new disciplinary tradition with an international reach, and in this he failed. World War I broke out, and within a year van Gennep was once again without a job. His journal closed down as well. Bitter Return to France: War, Closing Doors and Return to Unemployment Van Gennep was eventually ejected from Switzerland. Once again his stubbornness and refusal to relativize moral principles played a role. In October 1915 van Gennep was formally expelled from Switzerland because of his open criticism of the Swiss government and its pro-German attitudes during the war. Switzerland was officially a neutral country as World War I broke out, but it was apparent to everyone that the government secretly sided with Germany. That of course did not mean that one should say so in public. Van Gennep had an awful habit of criticizing authorities. He was thrown out of the country with a one-way ticket, and there was little his Swiss colleagues could do about it. Van Gennep’s only hope now was that he could get a job back in France. The Collège de France was out of the question, and he knew it. Less would suffice. He was willing to compromise. Whom should he contact? He had no patrons. He chose to write to Mauss, his former mentor, and asked for his support. It may from today’s perspective not seem the most obvious of choices, but Mauss was without doubt the person who knew van Gennep’s work most intimately and probably also the one who was most fully aware of his outstanding qualities as a social scientist. And van Gennep knew that Mauss knew. But Mauss also knew what Durkheim thought of van Gennep, especially after his devastating critique of The Elementary Forms of Religious Life in 1913. And in fact, Durkheim went Arnold van Gennep: Fragments of a Life-Work at the Thresholds 41 up against van Gennep as he put in his candidature for a position as Maîtrise de conférence in the province. We shall return to the details of the van Gennep- Durkheim controversy in the next chapter. It has to be said that getting established in French academia was not easy for anyone. One can here think of Mauss’ own assessment, made shortly after the war: ‘Young people today imagine things were easy before the war, with regular promotions and adequate compensation for work with no bumps along the road! What a mistake! I know of very few scholars whose lives back then were not difficult and full of sacrifice for a long period of time’ (Mauss in Fournier 2006: 83). However, for van Gennep it was this ‘period of sacrifice’ which would never come to an end. At the age of 43 he was without any clear future. And there is little doubt that this is one of those moments when he contemplated giving up on academia. In fact, World War I is sometimes identified as the period in which he started to turn to folklore. And it is not difficult to understand why so. At the same time, it is not an exact assessment. Van Gennep’s final abandonment of anthropology and sociology can be more precisely located to around 1924–1925. The Post-war Period: Doctorate title and Abandonment of Sociology and Ethnography: Arnold van Gennep as French Folklorist In 1915 van Gennep went back to France, expelled from Switzerland. He applied once again for teaching positions at French universities, but without being selected. As had happened before, and as would happen again, his open critique of Durkheim and his followers would cost him dear. Not knowing what to do, he for a short period started to teach at the lycée in Nice. In 1915 he was recalled by Poincaré to a post in the foreign office, where he remained until 1922. Once again employed full-time, and no doubt hugely disillusioned, van Gennep had too much academic energy to let go of everything. He still managed to advance his academic work, and kept opening his work towards new areas of interest. One of those interests was nationalism. He wrote several pieces on the war, again in ways that come very close to the writings of Marcel Mauss in the same period. He planned to write a three volume work on nationalism. He only managed to finish the first volume, a precious and totally overlooked contribution to the study of nationalism, published in 1922. In general, he kept on translating and publishing, and continued his editorial work for Mercure. On January 24 1921, at the age of 47, Arnold van Gennep became Docteur ès Lettres at the Sorbonne. He presented two works for the evaluation, two ‘theses’: his book from 1920 on totemism (L ́état actuel du problem totémique) and, as his minor, Rites of Passage. He received a ‘mention très honorable’; Levy-Bruhl and Bouglé were part of the six-man committee. 13 With hindsight, it is difficult to point 13 The fact of van Gennep’s doctoral degree, surprisingly often omitted in the literature on van Gennep, is still found reported in the Bulletin de la societé prehistorique francaise (a small circle in which van Gennep was held in high regard) 1921, issue 3, Liminality and the Modern 42 to two more substantial contributions ever presented for a title of Docteur. His book on totemism would in many ways pave the way for Lévi-Strauss’ structural approach to totemic classification, and Rites of Passage, here presented as a ‘minor’, is one of the most quoted anthropology books ever written. But in terms of securing jobs, it was all to no avail. In 1922 van Gennep had been invited to give a lecture tour in the United States and Canada, and off he went. He had asked
